content:
# John Chapter 7 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/JHN_007_page_001.jpg, JHN_007_page_002.jpg, JHN_007_page_003.jpg

### Jesus' Brothers Urge Him to Go Public

1. After this, Jesus stayed in Galilee. He deliberately avoided Judea because the Jewish leaders there were trying to kill him.

2. But the Jewish Festival of Shelters was approaching.

3. So his brothers said to him, "Leave here and go to Judea, where your followers can see the miracles you do!

4. You can't become a public figure if you hide like this. If you can do such wonderful things, show yourself to the world!"

5. For even his brothers didn't believe in him.

6. Jesus replied, "Now is not the right time for me to go, but for you any time is fine.

7. The world can't hate you, but it does hate me because I expose its evil deeds.

8. Go ahead and go to the festival. I am not going to this festival, because my time has not yet fully come."

9. After saying this, he stayed behind in Galilee.

### Jesus Teaches at the Festival

10. But after his brothers left for the festival, Jesus also went — not openly, but secretly.

11. The Jewish leaders were watching for him and asking, "Where is he?"

12. There was widespread whispering about him among the crowds. Some argued, "He's a good man," while others said, "No, he's deceiving the people."

13. But no one had the courage to speak publicly about him for fear of the authorities.

14. Not until halfway through the festival did Jesus go up to the temple courts and begin to teach.

15. The Jewish leaders were astonished. "How does he know so much when he's never been formally trained?" they asked.

16. So Jesus told them, "My teaching is not my own. It comes from the one who sent me.

17. Anyone who truly wants to do God's will can tell whether my teaching comes from God or whether I'm making it up.

18. Those who speak on their own authority are looking for personal glory, but someone who seeks to honor the one who sent him speaks the truth — there is nothing false about him.

19. Moses gave you the law, didn't he? Yet not one of you obeys it. So why are you trying to kill me?"

20. "You're demon-possessed!" the crowd retorted. "Who's trying to kill you?"

21. Jesus replied, "I did one miracle, and you're all stunned.

22. Moses gave you circumcision — though actually it didn't come from Moses but from the patriarchs — and you circumcise on the Sabbath.

23. Now if a child can be circumcised on the Sabbath so that the law of Moses is not broken, why are you furious with me for healing a man's whole body on the Sabbath?

24. Stop judging by appearances, and start judging fairly."

### Is Jesus the Messiah?

25. Some of the people of Jerusalem started to say, "Isn't this the man they're trying to kill?

26. Yet here he is, speaking openly, and they say nothing to him. Could it be that the leaders have concluded he really is the Messiah?

27. But we know where this man is from. When the Messiah comes, no one will know where he's from."

28. Then Jesus, still teaching in the temple courts, cried out, "Yes, you know me, and you know where I am from. But I have not come on my own. The one who sent me is real, and you don't know him.

29. But I know him, because I come from him and he sent me."

30. At this they tried to seize him, but no one could lay a hand on him, because his hour had not yet come.

31. Still, many in the crowd believed in him. "When the Messiah comes," they said, "will he perform more signs than this man has?"

32. The Pharisees heard the crowd whispering such things about him. Then the chief priests and the Pharisees sent temple guards to arrest him.

33. Jesus said, "I am with you for only a short time longer, and then I am going to the one who sent me.

34. You will look for me, but you will not find me; and where I am, you cannot come."

35. The Jewish leaders said to one another, "Where does this man plan to go that we can't find him? Is he going to go to the Jews scattered among the Greeks and teach the Greeks?

36. What did he mean when he said, 'You will look for me but won't find me,' and 'Where I am, you cannot come'?"

### Rivers of Living Water

37. On the last and greatest day of the festival, Jesus stood and shouted to the crowds, "Anyone who is thirsty may come to me and drink!

38. For the Scriptures declare that whoever believes in me, rivers of living water will flow from deep within them."

39. (When he said "living water," he was speaking of the Spirit, who would be given to everyone believing in him. But the Spirit had not yet been given, because Jesus had not yet entered into his glory.)

40. When the crowds heard him say this, some of them declared, "Surely this man is the Prophet."

41. Others said, "He is the Messiah." But still others argued, "The Messiah doesn't come from Galilee, does he?

42. Doesn't the Scripture say that the Messiah will be born of David's line and come from Bethlehem, the village where David lived?"

43. So the crowd was deeply divided over him.

44. Some wanted him arrested, but no one touched him.

### The Leaders Argue Over Jesus

45. When the temple guards returned without having arrested Jesus, the leading priests and Pharisees demanded, "Why didn't you bring him in?"

46. "We have never heard anyone speak like this!" the guards responded.

47. "Have you been fooled too?" the Pharisees retorted.

48. "Is there a single one of us rulers or Pharisees who believes in him?

49. This foolish crowd follows him, but they are ignorant of the law. God's curse is on them!"

50. Then Nicodemus, the leader who had visited Jesus earlier, spoke up.

51. "Is it legal to convict a man before he is given a hearing?" he asked.

52. They replied, "Are you from Galilee too? Search the Scriptures and see — no prophet comes from Galilee!"

[7:53-8:11 — The story of the woman caught in adultery does not appear in the Sinaiticus manuscript.]

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 8 — "I am not going":** Sinaiticus reads a flat "not" (οὐκ), not "not yet." Jesus says he's not going — then goes privately. This isn't deception. His brothers wanted a publicity tour (v.4). Jesus refuses that agenda. When he goes, it's on his own terms, in his own way, for his own purposes. Jesus frequently refuses to be managed — even by family.

**Verses 14-15 — "How does he know so much?":** The Greek literally asks how he "knows letters" — meaning formal scribal training. Jesus had never attended a rabbinic academy. His knowledge didn't come through institutional channels. This threatens the credentialing system. It always does. The authorities aren't questioning whether he's right — they're questioning his credentials to be right.

**Verse 24 — "Stop judging by appearances":** Jesus' sharpest ethical instruction in this chapter. The surface of things — credentials, Sabbath rules, geographic origins — keeps getting in the way of the deeper reality. The whole chapter is about surfaces versus depth: secret versus open travel, human knowledge versus divine origin, institutional authority versus true authority.

**Verses 37-39 — "Rivers of living water":** Jesus shouts this on the last day of Tabernacles, during the water-pouring ceremony — when the priest carries water from the Pool of Siloam and pours it at the base of the altar, while the crowd sings from Isaiah 12:3 ("With joy you will draw water from the wells of salvation"). Into this ritual, Jesus inserts himself: "Come to ME and drink." He doesn't improve the ceremony. He replaces it. The water is the Spirit — and the Spirit has not yet been given, because the cross has not yet happened. The gift waits for the giver to be glorified.

**Verses 40-43 — Division:** The crowd splits three ways: he's the Prophet, he's the Messiah, he can't be the Messiah because he's from Galilee. The irony is devastating — Jesus WAS born in Bethlehem (Matthew 2:1, Luke 2:4). They're using Scripture correctly but applying it to wrong information. Theological precision plus factual ignorance produces confident wrong answers. It always does.

**Verses 50-52 — Nicodemus speaks up:** The man who came to Jesus at night (ch. 3) now speaks in the daylight of the council chamber. He doesn't confess faith — he asks a legal question. But even this small act of fairness enrages the others. In polarized times, procedural fairness gets treated as partisanship. They attack Nicodemus's geography ("Are you from Galilee too?") because they can't attack his argument.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
